data.title: Downturn Coming? 2 Dependable Income Plays Retirees Can Count On If A Recession Hits
data.description:
Introduction

No matter what economists are saying regarding the state of the U.S. economy, I think the odds of a recession in the next year are increasing. Although experts currently have odds at less than 30%, I think odds are closer to 60% to 70% as we speak.

As a result, investors should be taking a look at their portfolios to see if they're positioned to withstand a potential downturn.

While the market is likely to see volatility in the near term, buying and holding stocks that are likely to continue providing income amidst uncertainty is prudent for investors, especially retirees.

In this article I discuss two holdings retirees should consider, their fundamentals, and why they are likely to perform well should the U.S. economy experience a potential downturn.

Why I Think a Recession May Be Closer Than We Think

Recent increases in unemployment, job revisions, weakness in commercial real estate, and projected GDP growth for Q3 revised lower—I think these are all indications of a potential recession.

In short, the economy doesn't appear as strong as it seems, and I think this is likely a reason the FED now appears dovish regarding interest rates.

Earlier this month, we saw a .25 basis point cut, with more expected by year's end. Sure, many have been calling for a recession the past few years, but this time I think one is highly likely, despite probability around 29%.

Below you can see that since 1950, every time unemployment has increased dramatically, it has been followed by a recession (gray shaded areas). Currently, unemployment sits at 4.3%, and this would likely have to increase further before being considered recession territory.

I think Americans' reliance on credit cards in the past few years is also an indication of economic weakening. Since inflation rose rapidly in 2022, so did credit card balances. And with the Fed's tightening cycle, I think the economy may be finally starting to show cracks as a result.

Total credit card debt in the U.S. in the second quarter was $1.209 trillion, up from $1.182 trillion in the first quarter. While this did decline modestly from Q4'24's $1.211 trillion, this was $282 billion higher than the fourth quarter prior to COVID.

Although this doesn't necessarily suggest we may see a recession, further rises in unemployment in the coming months are likely to push us into one. And while some investors may prefer to sit on the sidelines in cash, I think it's best to take advantage of mispriced stocks and dollar-cost average. So, let's get into these two positions.

#1 Agree Realty (ADC)—Best-in-Class Portfolio

Agree Realty is one of the best net lease REITs an investor can buy, in my opinion. The stock is in the red in the past year, thanks to the pullback it has experienced in recent months. Although REITs are expected to see tailwinds from lower base rates, many saw their share prices decline after the FED's .25 basis point cut.

I think this has nothing to do with the companies themselves but more to do with macro uncertainty. I think this is also a reason the precious metal gold continues to soar to new highs.

In April of this year, Agree Realty made a new 52-week high around $80 a share and currently sits around $71 a share, making them attractively priced if you're a long-term investor. This puts their forward P/AFFO multiple at 16.49x.

Aside from the pullback, ADC's monthly dividend is backed by some of the strongest fundamentals. And this is evident by the company's credit rating upgrade in August by Fitch. Now sporting an A credit rating, this gives the REIT cost of capital advantages over smaller peers with lower credit ratings.

This also puts them in a favorable position to make accretive acquisitions, especially since M&A activity will likely increase due to lower base rates. But what's most attractive about Agree Realty is their recession-resistant portfolio.

Currently, the REIT's exposure to investment-rated tenants like Walmart (WMT), Home Depot (HD), Lowe's (LOW), Tractor Supply Company (TSCO), and AutoZone (AZO) makes them a viable option for a potential downturn.

Most of Agree Realty's rent collection comes from grocery, home improvement, and tire & auto service centers. While home improvement stores may see modest declines in a recession as consumers become more financially conscious, grocery stores and tire & auto service centers will likely see business as usual.

This is because consumers still need necessities like food, water, and their vehicles to do things like go to work and run errands.

Another reason Agree Realty is one of the best-positioned REITs for a potential downturn is their best-in-class balance sheet. The company has virtually no debt maturing for the next few years, putting them in a comfortable position to make acquisitions and withstand any economic volatility.

With their low leverage and robust liquidity of over $2 billion, ADC should be a clear choice for investors looking for stability and income reliability.

And lastly, the REIT pays a monthly dividend that's well-covered with growing cash flows and adjusted funds from operations. At the end of their latest quarter, their AFFO payout ratio was a conservative 71.2%.

And free cash flow after dividends was projected to be 15% higher than the previous year, around $120 million.

#2 USCF Gold Strategy Plus Income Fund ETF (USG) - Potential Outperformance While Providing Income

The second holding retirees should consider is USG. While Agree Realty has underperformed in the past year, slightly in the red, gold has been on a tear as macro uncertainty remains high. And going forward, I think gold's performance is likely to continue, at least for the near to medium term.

As a result, this could result in strong capital appreciation for investors holding the fund inside their portfolio. USG looks to track the long-term performance of gold by selling call options against gold and gold futures. This, and collateral interest income, is how the fund provides (income).

Below is a list of USG's holdings. The ETF also sells gold warrants occasionally, up to 25% through its subsidiary. These can juice gains but also enhance losses. Unlike ADC, USG pays on a quarterly basis instead of monthly.

Additionally, USG has an attractive starting yield of 6%. So, not only do you get the potential for outperformance, but you collect a solid starting yield as well.

USG doesn't have a super long track record, with an inception date of November 3, 2021, so not quite four years. Below are charts of how USG has performed against Vanguard's S&P 500 ETF (VOO) over the past 1, 3, and since inception.

1 year:

3 years:

Since Inception:

Over a 1, 3, and nearly 4-year period, USG has outperformed VOO in total returns. The closest in terms of performance was in the past 3 years. But USG still comfortably outperformed VOO, up 94.10% at the time of writing compared to 78.41% for the latter.

And we all know past performance is no indicator of future performance, but while macro uncertainty is likely to remain in the near future, USG's chances of continuing to outperform remain high.

Furthermore, the fund has seen solid NAV growth since inception, another reason for its outperformance. Since 2021, the ETF has experienced NAV growth of 69.25%. And as you can see, their share price has followed suit, up 69.31% over the same period.

Why ADC & USG for Retirees?

Because dividends paid by REITs are taxed as ordinary income, this makes their distributions less tax-efficient and better held inside a tax-advantaged account like a Roth IRA.

USG's distributions are also taxed as ordinary income, so they also are best held in a tax-advantaged account. So, retirees can withdraw their distributions from both holdings tax-free.

Risks & Takeaway

Agree Realty could continue to underperform, especially if the U.S. treasuries remain volatile. One reason is because shortly after the Fed's rate cut, long-term rates declined, only to reverse shortly afterward.

However, ADC maintains a comfortable position despite the likelihood to see increased short-term volatility. But a severe recession could result in a decline in portfolio occupancy, impacting their financials and leading to further downside.

USG's relatively short track record could be viewed as risky, as the fund could underperform over the long term, especially if the U.S. dollar strengthens. And if the market experiences heavy volatility, the fund could see lower distributions as a result, making them riskier if seeking income stability.

But for the near to medium term, I expect continued outperformance, as geopolitical risks and economic uncertainty will likely result in a strong performance for gold & gold futures.

If you're a retiree looking for holdings to strengthen your portfolio while providing income stability as well as reliability, then consider Agree Realty for monthly income & USCF's Gold Strategy Plus Income Fund ETF for your income and potential outperformance.

data.title: Kingstone Companies: Disciplined Underwriting Capabilities With Visible Growth Catalysts
data.description:
Intro

Kingstone Companies (NASDAQ:KINS) is a regional property and casualty insurance provider that operates primarily in New York. Our investment thesis lies in the fact that many larger competitors are leaving the region, which will allow Kingstone to capture market share. This will propel the company to have significantly larger earnings growth relative to peers. The company has been in the process of a strategic repositioning lately as they continue exiting non-core lines to focus on homeowners. This narrowed focus allows them to understand the market and begin deploying capital more efficiently.

As a part of this new strategy, the company has created a new Select homeowners product, which aims to use more innovative methods to ensure underwriting profitability. Their new product uses by-peril pricing, insurance score integrations, and updated deductible data in order to estimate potential losses more accurately, which has demonstrated ~31% lower claim frequency than past products. Another byproduct of this restructuring has been the trend of strong quarterly declines in their expense ratio. Since 2021, they have managed to reduce it from 45.98 to 36.26 as of Q2 FY2025.

On top of this, the company has diversified its reinsurance sources by issuing its first catastrophe bond. When investors own this bond, they receive a high-interest rate, but when a qualifying catastrophe occurs, the bond principal is used to pay for the insurance claims. By creating this bond, the company has further diversified their sources of reinsurance, which should reduce volatility in losses. Currently, competitors like Adirondack Insurance Exchange and AmGUARD have reduced their exposure or exited entirely from the NY homeowners business. This creates a very strong opportunity for Kingstone to capture this market share, especially as these restructuring efforts continue underway.

Luckily, Kingstone has struck a deal with AmGUARD for renewal rights, meaning Kingstone gets the first shot at offering replacement coverage to all of those policyholders that will be losing coverage. This agreement covers about $70 million of written premiums, meaning if Kingstone converts 100% of those policies, their revenue base could jump by more than 30%. Overall, there is a strong investment thesis here, as the company is clearly making efforts to improve the quality and stability of their business while expanding into new markets due to the leave of competition. Taken together, these factors allow Kingstone to seriously expand earnings while trading at a steep discount in comparison to peers.

Financial Snapshot

The company had a record quarter in Q2 FY2025, with net income sitting at $11.3M (~+149% YoY) and diluted EPS at $0.78 (~+111% YoY). They also achieved a combined ratio of 71.5% (~-6.7 pts YoY) which is very strong, as industry averages are closer to 95-100%. Their insurance premiums have been growing well, with net premium earnings up 52% YoY and core direct written premiums up 16.6% YoY for the quarter. These are all signs that the company's restructuring is translating meaningfully into improved results. Their exceptional combined ratio can be interpreted as a clear sign of their underwriting prowess. As of Q2 FY 2025 the company had an ROE of 40.61, which is exceptionally high for the industry. According to data from Seeking Alpha, the sector median for ROE was 10.78, meaning KINS sits significantly above peers from a profitability standpoint. It is very rare to see both scale and efficiency outperforming at once and creating a very strong fundamental basis for the company. Management has provided guidance for FY2025 with net premium earnings estimated to be ~$187M, net combined ratio at 79-83%, diluted EPS at $1.95-$2.35, and ROE at 30-38%. These provide us some hard benchmarks we can utilize for modeling their growth going into FY2026, and FY2027 .

Market Opportunity

Before we can begin modeling Kingstone, we need to first evaluate and estimate how much market share the company can possibly capture. The immediate opportunity that arises is the AmGUARD Renewal Rights deal, which covers ~$70M of written premiums. The fact of the matter is that Kingstone will not retain 100% of these customers. Based on typical renewal rights transactions, we can estimate about a 40% conversion in the low case, 55% in the base case, and over 70% for the high case. This means that even in the low case, we can estimate ~+15% in Kingstone's FY2025 net premiums earned. Management has estimated ~$12M will be earned starting September of 2025 as policies begin their rollout. The remaining gains in the transaction will be earned throughout the next 12 months.

It's also important to note that Kingstone will selectively choose which policies to pursue to ensure the risk profile fits within their "Select" product line. Evidence suggests that large national carriers have been actively shifting capital towards higher-margin lines such as commercial and specialty, which gives unique opportunities for niche providers such as Kingstone. It's also clear that management has their sights set on growing their premiums significantly, as they have stated a goal of over $500M in premiums within the next 5 years. This seems to be a realistic goal given the number of carriers losing interest in the New York area, which will potentially allow Kingstone to swallow up some additional renewal-rights agreements within the next few years.

On September 24th, management announced plans to launch its expansion outside of the New York region beginning in 2026. This represents another strategic inflection point, as Kingstone is now evolving into a multi-state operator. The 2026 expectancy signals confidence that growth will be sustained after the AmGUARD renewal rights deal is no longer active. If Kingstone manages to continue growing their market share at this rate, our implied earnings profile should prove very conservative. In addition to this, the company also issued FY2026 guidance of diluted EPS at $2.10-2.80, net premium earned of ~$233M, and ROE at 26-36%. This would mean that for FY2026, the companies forward P/E ratio would be 6-7x, which adds further confluence to our case. Investors should view this expansion as extremely positive, as it provides a structural growth catalyst that could fuel the company's growth for years.

Valuation Model

Scenario Net Premium Earned AmGUARD Conversion Combined Ratio EPS Estimate
Bull $235-245M 70% (~$49M) 78-80% $2.80-3.00
Base ~$224M 55% (~$38M) 81–82% $2.40–2.60
Bear $210–215M 40% (~$28M) 85–87% $1.70–2.00

Assumptions: We anchor our estimates to management's guidance for FY2025. We then consider the extra income coming from the AmGUARD deal with a varying conversion rate for each of our cases. Our estimates for combined ratio fall in line near management's guidance with some variance for each case. Our estimates for EPS fall in line with FY2025 guidance of $1.95-2.35 with incremental lifts depending on the premiums earned from the AmGUARD deal.

Scenario EPS Estimate 8x P/E 10x P/E 12x P/E
Bull $2.80-3.00 $22-24 $28-30 $34-36
Base $2.40-2.60 $19-21 $24-26 $29-31
Bear $1.70-2.00 $14-16 $17-20 $20-24

Above is a table that presents varying P/E multiples and the estimated share price for each. According to data from Seeking Alpha, the sector median is 12.06, which would mean a fair valuation in our base case would yield a share price of $29-31 (~+105.33% from today's share price).

Risks & Catalysts

Although Kingstone is well positioned for sustained earnings growth, like every investment, there are a few risks. Kingstone has significant exposure to downstate New York, which opens them up to many risks from natural disasters. Although their reinsurance tower and cat bond are likely to protect solvency, any major event is likely to still significantly affect earnings. Beyond this, the other main risk is poor conversion in their AmGUARD deal. If conversion rates end up closer to 40%, then EPS growth will definitely be stunted. However, it is possible for Kingstone to also absorb some of the policies from other competition even if they do not have a direct deal with them.

In the near term, it will be very important to watch quarterly disclosures to monitor conversion rates. Over the past few quarters, their net expense ratio has fallen significantly. If this trend continues, it will provide incremental increases to EPS even without premium growth. Lastly, it would be very beneficial if the company continues migrating more of their policies to their Select products. These policies lead to significantly lower loss costs and promote their long track record of positive underwriting.

Conclusion

Kingstone is a niche NY homeowners insurance company that is capturing incrementally larger market share as competition continues to leave the region. The company is guaranteed to absorb some of these policies through their exclusive deal with AmGUARD. On top of this, the company has established a stable reinsurance tower with a catastrophe bond in order to reduce volatility and protect the company's solvency in light of disaster. At the same time, they have been shifting their policies towards their Select line, which provides significantly lower losses due to stronger data analysis. As they continue to improve their expense ratio, these factors combined will lead to serious gains in profitability. If we value the company in accordance with the sector median, the implied share price is $29-31, which is currently ~105.33% above today's share price.

The main falsifiers of our thesis would be any lags in conversion rates or any major disasters that could lead to earnings taking a hit. However, the combination of strong underwriting ability, a stable reinsurance base, and tangible market share captures strongly outweighs these risks. In short, it is clear that Kingstone is an undervalued niche insurance company with identifiable catalysts that should lead to exceptional earnings for many quarters to come.

data.title: Viking Therapeutics: A Huge Reward Or A Total Loss
data.description:
Introduction

I had never heard about Viking Therapeutics (NASDAQ:VKTX) until I got interested in drug obesity stocks, which had happened very recently. Since then, I broke down Novo Nordisk (NVO) stock twice, and the second time I bought it myself. After opening a position in Novo, I decided to take a look at Eli Lilly (LLY) and started a very small position there to mitigate Novo’s falls. And of course, now I am curious about the smaller names, especially in cheap ones like Viking.

The company’s lead program is called VK2735, a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist. That might sound technical, but for anyone who follows the space, it basically means they’re playing in the same field as Lilly’s tirzepatide (Zepbound/Mounjaro) and Novo’s semaglutide (Wegovy/Ozempic). The interesting part is that Viking is running development on two versions of the same molecule: one injectable, one oral. The management explains it could make a great competitive advantage, because patients could start with an injectable to lose weight quickly and then transition to an oral pill for long-term maintenance, and since both are the same drug, that transition might be smoother than what Novo or Lilly could offer with their separate molecules.

This is, without a doubt, a nice store, but after breaking it all down, I found it more nuanced. Lilly and Novo are miles ahead in development. They already have products on the market, and their oral candidates are in late-stage trials (Stage 3 Lilly) or regulatory review (Novo). Viking is just starting Phase III for the injectable and is still in Phase II for the oral. Another problem is that Viking’s molecule could prove to be harder to tolerate or less effective, which would immediately cancel any positive competitive effect of the same molecule.

Even though it doesn’t sound great at the moment, I see a possibility for an opportunity here, and I’ll talk about it at the end. But for now, you have to understand what you are getting into, because the risk is just enormous.

The Sell-Off

I have to start from the sell-off because this was the reason I noticed the stock. And I am sure I wasn't the only one. Not often do you see a company that plunges over 40% in a day. In August, Viking released results from its Phase II trial of the oral VK2735. The data wasn’t that bad. The treatment worked - patients lost up to about 12% of their body weight in just 13 weeks, which is competitive enough, and it technically met the trial’s endpoints. But no one expected dropout rates to be that huge, and it became the driver of the selloff.

Depending on the dose, between 20% and 38% of patients discontinued treatment early, compared with 18% on placebo. That is a huge red flag because if people can’t tolerate the drug for 13 weeks, how will they manage 64 or 72 weeks, which is the standard for Phase III obesity programs? The whole point of GLP-1 drugs is not just that they work fast but that people stay on them for months, even years, generating recurring revenue for companies. A drug that causes one-third of patients to quit early is not going to compete well in that environment.

Besides, if we look at the side effects, it doesn’t look very good either. At high doses, nausea was reported by as many as 38%-55% of patients, vomiting by about 35%, diarrhea by up to 25%, and constipation by as many as 43%. To put this into perspective, Lilly’s orforglipron in Phase III had discontinuation rates due to side effects in the 6–11% range over 72 weeks, while placebo was around 5%. In Viking’s case, they were already hitting 20–38% in just 13 weeks. So, of course, looking at the comparison, people were disappointed, to put it lightly. Not only is the company way behind in timing, but the results are not perfect. But was the sell-off of over 40% justified? Not necessarily.

Interestingly, the data was not so one-sided. One weird part of the Viking trial is that even the placebo group had very high discontinuation and side effect rates. For example, 48% of placebo patients reported nausea, and 18% dropped out of the trial. That suggests there could be a strong psychological element, or maybe some mistakes in how the trial was run. Trials like that are really tricky, and I have actually studied the course of how to make the study so the results will be statistically adequate. So many details and nuances could ruin it. The difference between placebo nausea (48%) and treatment nausea (58%) is still significant, but it’s not as extreme as it looks if you just take the first number.

It’s also important to remember that this was only a Phase II trial. Phase II studies are often used to test different dosing, and companies usually use trial two results to adjust these in Phase III to improve tolerability. Viking itself has said it will slow down titration in later studies to minimize gastrointestinal side effects. So the Phase III data could look better, but until then, all investors can go on is what’s in front of them, and right now, what’s in front of them is weaker than Lilly’s and Novo’s results.

Competition

If we really compare Viking to Lilly or Novo, it’s very hard to see positives in Viking. These two giants are way ahead, and there is basically no category in which Viking could potentially win besides the easier transition from injectables to oral treatments, but this is not a criterion for the FDA approval, and the management confirmed so as well. They will need to show strong results and lower dropout rates. There is no other way.

We think the #1 fundamental that both patients and clinicians want is efficacy. Everybody wants the most effective weight-loss drug. And so you have to expect that and really be able to compete on that. And that's why I think our study, with the doses we selected and the profile we've seen with the drug so far, should be highly competitive on efficacy.

I think second then, tolerability becomes more important following efficacy, and then learning how to properly titrate somebody is an important element of that. And how we see us entering the market, I think the 2735 molecule will be very competitive based on the profile we've seen thus far.

The competition is a major risk factor because Lilly has tirzepatide, sold as Zepbound for obesity and Mounjaro for diabetes, which already brings in billions. They’re way ahead in oral GLP-1 (orforglipron), and it looks like they will file it soon. On top of that, they’re developing retatrutide, a triple agonist (GLP-1, GIP, and glucagon) that could be even more powerful, and it’s already in late-stage trials.

Novo was the first one in the market with Wegovy and Ozempic. Their oral version of semaglutide has already shown 14–16% weight loss in Phase III and is under FDA review. Basically, it looks like Novo will be the first one to achieve approval, and it could be a major positive catalyst for NVO in 2026, which could send all of the competitors, including LLY and VKTX, falling. Beyond that, they’re working on CagriSema (a combination of cagrilintide and semaglutide) and Amycretin (an amylin + GLP-1 co-agonist), both of which are moving into Phase III and could redefine the space again.

And then there’s Viking. Their VK2735 (injectable) has only just entered Phase III, their oral version is still in Phase II with questionable tolerability, and their amylin program is only pre-IND. Again, the only advantage is the same molecule, and they talk about maintenance a lot, but Lilly and Novo are also talking about oral therapy as a maintenance strategy, so it’s not truly a competitive advantage. If Viking’s molecule is less tolerable, no one will care that the transition is easier.

The NASH (MASH) Angle

One area where Viking could prove to be not too far behind is liver treatment. Their drug VK2809 is a thyroid receptor agonist being developed for NASH (non-alcoholic steatohepatitis) or MASH (metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis), a serious liver condition that is often a problem in obese people and tied to metabolic syndrome.

This field suddenly got a lot more interesting in September when Roche announced it was buying 89bio for up to $3.5 billion to get its MASH developments. At the same time, the FDA said it would accept a new, non-invasive biomarker called liver stiffness measurement as a surrogate endpoint for MASH trials. That is a big deal because it removes the need for invasive liver biopsies and could accelerate trial timelines across the industry, but there is a slight problem again.

Madrigal already has the first FDA-approved MASH drug on the market, and Novo got Wegovy recently approved for MASH. Viking’s program VK2809 could become more valuable, but again, as MASH is closely tied to obesity, I would expect doctors’ preference to go for the names like Wegovy that are approved to treat both obesity and MASH simultaneously.

Financials

Here I have found something unexpectedly positive. Viking and its investors don’t have to worry that this company is going bankrupt any time soon. As of June 2025, the company reported $808 million in cash and short-term investments. Their R&D spend is about $60 million per quarter, with total operating expenses around $75 million. That means they have more than two years of runway without raising more money, which is a luxury for a small biotech. They will be able to finish Phase III with that cash position. They have almost no debt.

Their market cap is tiny. It’s at around $2.8 billion. Lilly is at $650+ billion, and Novo is at $250+ billion. When put this way, you could easily justify the company being behind those giants. After all, they don’t need to capture the entire market. They only need to get approval and be an additional player on the market that is expected to be enormous. On the one hand, it means they can’t possibly compete on sales infrastructure or marketing power. But at the same time, the stock can move violently in response to good news. If they get good Phase III results, the upside could be enormous. Much bigger than those names that already had their rallies.

The Risk/Reward Setup

So I guess we can agree that Viking is a very high-risk, but also potentially high-reward stock.

On the risk side, their oral program looks inferior to Lilly’s and Novo’s. Their Phase II dropout rates were alarming. Their Phase III injectables could still disappoint. And like many biotechs, there’s always the risk they simply never get a product approved. There is absolutely no way of being sure the company will ever achieve the approval. You have to understand it.

On the reward side, the stock is small enough that even a small win could send it soaring. If Phase III results come in better than expected, or if their MASH drug shows strong data, or if a big pharma player decides to buy them like Roche did with 89bio, then shareholders could make nice returns. Who could have bought them? Definitely not Lilly and Novo, which are way before in all of the developments. There is simply no reason for them to spend the money. Maybe some other pharma that wasn’t working on obesity, but would like to start.

This is in no way a safe stock. It’s not even a growth stock. It’s essentially a call option on obesity and MASH, with the possibility of total loss if the programs fail. For the right investor/trader, that can make sense. For most of us, it’s probably too risky.

Conclusion

Right now, Viking doesn’t lead in anything compared to Lilly and Novo. Their only “advantage” is having the same molecule in oral and injectable forms, but it may not matter if patients can’t tolerate the drug. The company is well-funded, it has a potentially valuable MASH program, and its market cap is small enough that good news could create big upside. But this is a very speculative play, like most of biopharma. So many of them simply fall apart and never make anything meaningful.

I wanted safety and proven results, so I bought Novo or Lilly. They are not only ahead, but they also have enormous resources to continue research and development, which I really like. If you want to speculate on a small biotech that could either blow up completely or get bought out at a premium, Viking is your stock. Just don’t confuse it for anything else. This is a high-risk, high-reward play, the kind of thing you size like an option and accept that it could go to zero.

data.title: Tesla: A Better 3Q Deliveries Won't Likely Fix Stock's Front-Load Bubble
data.description:
Real or Just Another Round Trip?

Tesla, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:TSLA) is approaching a new all-time high before its 3Q 2025 deliveries next week, as market believes the number can beat the consensus. This bull run has been further amplified by Elon Musk's $1 billion stock purchase, along with the broader AI rally in U.S. equities.

TSLA may seem like a no-brainer to make money for holding the stock in the past five years. You buy into the rally and then using future prospects to justify the rally. But we shouldn't forget the stock had many big round trips. For example, the stock rallied 100% in two months in late 2024 and then dropped more than 50% just three months later, wiping out all those gains.

Is the rally sustainable or just another head fake? Keep in mind, the gap between price and fundamentals (including revenue, earnings, and FCF) is widening to a very lofty level. Even though the YoY deliveries could rebound in 3Q 2025, we are still far from seeing a real inflection. We need at least two blockbuster earnings reports to confirm growth reacceleration. Until then, I stick to my Strong Sell rating.

However, despite trading at a 250x forward P/E, TSLA can still be traded due to its elevated volatility. The real danger is acting like a momentum trader by chasing the rally while convincing yourself it's fundamentally driven. I believe the market is speculating on TSLA's future AI growth opportunities, which is why the stock can swing a lot in both directions.

Expectation vs. Speculation

Many people believe that they've built their fortune by picking the "right" stock, such as TSLA, and think that holding it forever will continue to reward them as it did in the past. This can likely reflect confirmation bias and availability bias in investment decisions.

I remember Michael Covel's The Complete Turtle Trader said, "the Trend is your friend until the end when it bends". Buying TSLA stock at this level is not wrong if you are a pure trend follower.

However, we should be careful not to confuse a bullish trend with the idea that TSLA is fundamentally strong. There is a big difference between expectation and speculation.

As I mentioned in my 2Q FY2025 earnings analysis, TSLA's deliveries have been declining on YoY basis for many quarters. Both revenue and earnings continue to fall, with margins contracting. The company's FCF has also been declining. TSLA trying to sell its long-term growth opportunity through autonomous driving and "physical AI", including Robotaxi and robotics platforms, rather than its core car business, which has been doing poorly due to heavy competition. People are speculating that its FSD and AI robotics will capture huge market share, which I believe is fully priced in.

According to a latest report, Tesla's U.S market share has dropped to the lowest since 2017, holding only 38% of the EV market. In Europe, TSLA is also facing fierce competition. We saw BYD (BYD) was selling more cars than TSLA in May 2025. In China, despite a recent rebound in demand, YTD deliveries are still down 5.9% YoY. At the same time, domestic EV brands are rising and becoming increasingly competitive, posing a bigger threat to TSLA's recovery. Nevertheless, its total 3Q FY2025 deliveries will likely come in ahead of consensus next week in early October, supported by recent positive data. However, I don't expect the upside surprise to be significant, and it could even trigger a "sell the news" reaction after the strong stock rally.

Even if TSLA can achieve a growth rebound in 3Q 2025 (delivering more than 463,000 vehicles), it does mean the company has achieved a real growth inflection, because the positive surprise could be driven by a demand pulled forward ahead of the EV tax credit deadline on September 30th.

Lastly, the market appears more focused on speculation that TSLA's future revenue will come largely from robotics and broad-based FSD deployment. However, some may also speculate that TSLA's fate could change dramatically under a different and "unfriendly" administration three years from now.

Does Elon Musk's $1 Billion Stock Purchase Mean Anything?

From a sentiment perspective, the CEO Elon Musk recently bought $1 billion worth of its stock at elevated levels (close to $400), which can indeed deliver some long-term bullish signals, pushing the stock higher. At the same time, many analysts upgrading the stock and raising their price targets, which is way ahead of itself in my view. For example, an analyst from Piper Sandler noted, "We think TSLA's multiple is so high because the company is using AI to disrupt markets that are almost unimaginably large." He also justifies the current 250x P/E as a "front load" move.

The first question is when the company will actually use AI to disrupt markets in the future. Don't just say "eventually it will". If it does happen, how significant will it be? The reality is we don't know the timeline, especially since Elon has already failed to fulfill his promise of 50% CAGR in deliveries made several years ago.

A reasonable expectation should be centered on data, at least one blockbuster quarter to validate this optimism. Otherwise, it's just a speculation that could create huge downside risk if it fails to materialize. We also need to know how much this "front loading" has already priced in, given the stock's unrealistic valuation.

TSLA is in a Huge Bubble, and Getting Bigger

Some argue that TSLA is not in a bubble because the company could generate tremendous growth in the future, even though its car business is declining. Sure, stock prices are forward-looking. And yes, a significant growth rebound can justify valuation expansion.

I want to share a few examples to show how a fundamental rally looks like. NVIDIA (NVDA)'s stock jumped higher since January 2024, after delivering a blockbuster 2Q FY2024 earnings including 100% YoY revenue growth and 425% YoY EPS growth. Some people also argue that Palantir (PLTR) is in a bubble, trading at 280x of non-GAAP P/E fwd. At least PLTR's revenue growth accelerated from 27% YoY in 2Q FY2024 to 48% YoY in 2Q FY2025, showing clear upside momentum.

Based on the FY2025 consensus, TSLA's revenue is expected to decline 5% YoY, and its EPS could drop 30% YoY. In addition, as shown on the chart above, Street analysts have not raised earnings estimates, signaling no near-term growth rebound. Its P/E is expected to normalize to 30x by 2030, which shows a massive "front load". Therefore, I believe TSLA is in a huge bubble, with downside risk far greater than upside in the long term.

Trade it, Don't Own It

Since my analysis is focused on fundamentals (not technical analysis), I've maintained a Strong Sell rating on TSLA for many quarters, including this one. That doesn't mean you can't profit by trading it. It may sound easy to say that when TSLA jumps higher, you chase it, and when the trend reverses, you short it. But in practice, that is not an easy game to win consistently in the long term.

For fundamental investors holding TSLA long term, you should ask yourself: when you make money, is it because you simply got lucky to ride the bubble rally and then found a reason to justify it (confirmation bias)? Anyone can do that. But when the price turns down by 50%, you will need another reason to justify why the stock could go down that much, because I don't see any fundamentals to support it.

data.title: Zebra Technologies: Appeal Is Slightly On The Increase
data.description:
Over the summer, I questioned if shares of Zebra Technologies (NASDAQ:ZBRA) offered asset intelligence, yet I wondered if it offered investor intelligence as well. Since that view in July, shares have fallen by high single digits, while the market at large has been setting fresh highs.

This of course came amidst an eventful summer, including quarterly earnings, but moreover a substantial deal being announced as well. The earnings results looked sound, as the same conclusion applies to the M&A deal. Amidst demanding valuations from the get-go early in the summer, the risk-reward has improved, but is not necessary extremely compelling yet.

Enterprise Asset Intelligence

Zebra Technologies is a provider of EAI solutions, which includes a whole range of products, software, and services. These are used for automatic identification and capturing of data.

These are used to connect people with assets in an intelligent manner in a wide range of applications, including retail, logistics, e-commerce, manufacturing, transportation, and frankly many more industries and applications. Such applications and products include mobile computing, specialty, printing, barcode scanning, RFID reading and scanning.

Zebra generates about $5 billion in sales generated from over 100 facilities, which are located across the globe. The company employs about 10,000 workers and is quite R&D intensive, with over 10% of sales spent on R&D efforts, manifested in thousands of patents being held by the business.

The company has seen quite solid growth pre-pandemic, as revenues had risen from $3.6 billion in 2015 to a peak around $5.8 billion in 2022, as the boom resulted in operating margins peaking around 20% of sales. After revenues peaked in 2022, when margins already came down from their peak, reality really kicked in back in 2023. Sales fell to $4.6 billion in 2023, as operating profits were essentially cut in half to just over half a billion.

Peak profits around $17 per share fell to the $10 per share mark, yet the company guided for stability in 2024.

Recent Trends

After shares fell to a low around the $200 mark late in 2023, the company saw shares rally to a high around the $400 mark late in 2024. Following a tough 2023, revenues rose by 9% to $4.98 billion in 2024, as full-year margins recovered some three points, allowing adjusted earnings to rise some 37% to $13.52 per share. After accounting for stock-based compensation expenses, earnings came in closer to $12 per share.

Momentum was accelerating through the year, with fourth-quarter sales up some 31% on the year before. Nonetheless, the outlook for 2025 was much more muted, with sales seen up some 5%, as adjusted earnings were seen moving up towards $15 per share.

In April, the company reported a solid 11% increase in first-quarter sales, and while the company maintained the sales guidance, the EBITDA guidance was cut due to anticipated tariffs, with adjusted earnings seen around $14.25 per share, down seventy-five cents from the original guidance, but still up from 2024. With stock-based compensation expenses being up, I pegged realistic earnings to levels down to just $11-$12 per share.

Trading at $328 per share, shares traded at 27 times realistic earnings around $12 per share, which frankly is quite a demanding multiple in my view. This is the case, even as the business is far removed from peak performance, but frankly might be close to performing around its long term average potential, in terms of sales and margin potential.

This made me quite reserved, alongside the rapid technological changes taking place, which might as well accelerate demand for its products and technologies, but might also render some solutions useless, or invite non-domain competition into its field.

Believing that the valuation was too demanding to see a compelling risk-reward, I was erring on the cautious side here.

A Soft Summer

Since July, shares of Zebra have fallen from $328 to current levels around the $300 mark. This can be attributed to the news events early in August, when Zebra announced its second quarter results, with this news being accompanied by a substantial M&A transition.

Second quarter sales rose just over 6% to $1.29 billion and while this percentage growth was slower than the first quarter, the results were strong enough for the business to raise both the sales and earnings guidance, also aided by the softer dollar. The company now sees full year sales up 5-7%, with adjusted earnings now seen at $15.50 per share, plus or minus twenty-five cents. Fortunately, stock-based compensation expenses now trend a bit lower at $180 million, still equal to nearly $3 per share after taxes. This still makes for a $12.50 realistic earnings per share number.

The 51 million shares are now valued at $15.3 billion at the $300 mark, this valuation excluding a $1.3 billion net debt load, for a $16.6 billion enterprise valuation. With revenues seen around $5.3 billion, the business is valued around 3.1 times sales, with realistic earnings multiples coming in around 24 times earnings, all while EBITDA comfortably tops the billion mark. While such multiples are in line with historical averages and peers, it comes at a premium compared to overall market multiples in the high-teens, or near 20 times.

A net debt load of $1.3 billion is set to double, as the company announced a $1.3 billion cash deal to acquire Elo Touch Solutions, an innovator of solutions that accelerated automation in retail, hospitality, healthcare and QSR restaurants. With a $400 million sales contribution, the resulting 3.2 times sales multiple is at par to its own valuation, with similar EBITDA margins reported here.

This suggests an $80 million EBITDA contribution, more or less, making a $1.1-$1.2 billion pro forma EBITDA number likely, which makes it likely that leverage ratios are seen below 2.5 times in all likelihood. The company targets $25 million in EBITDA synergies by year three, being a substantial and valuable target and contribution, if achieved.

And Now?

I believe that the deal for Elo looks largely fair, and valued at around 8% of the enterprise valuation of Zebra, the company will make a similar revenue contribution. This makes for a solid contribution, and while leverage will tick up a bit, I see no major issues on this front.

The realistic earnings multiples have come down a bit, in between the decline in the share price and modest hike in the full-year guidance, yet amidst still elevated stock-based compensation expenses, realistic earnings are still trending around the $12.50 per share mark in my view, with upside to that number pending the deal with Elo and continued growth.

In between this, I am turning more constructive on the risk-reward here, amidst a modest pullback in the share price, a resilient performance, and a fair acquisition. However, at a near mid-twenty times earnings multiple, valuations are not very cheap even though they are in line with historical averages, yet appeal is certainly on the increase here. This makes me willing to buy potential pullbacks in the $275s range, as a modest premium to the market seems fully justified here.

data.title: Avino Silver & Gold: From Junior To Intermediate Producer In The Making, Still Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

Avino Silver & Gold Mines (NYSE:ASM) is up ~27% since I called them a buy back in late July, and despite the significant stock price appreciation seen in recent years, I believe the previous title where I said they are “Planning To Triple Production, Yet Still Flying Under-The-Radar” still remains valid.

There have been very important internal and macro developments since covering them, which I believe warrant an update to the thesis and valuation, so let’s look into them again.

Recent Quarter

Over the past quarter, the company has reported very solid developments, lowering their cash costs and reaching a positive $4.4 million free cash flow, which includes the CAPEX for their La Preciosa project, which should ramp up significantly starting next year, as you can see below.

Back at the end of August, they also acquired 100% ownership of the La Preciosa project in Mexico from Deterra Royalties for $22 million, including a $13.25 million upfront payment followed by an $8.75 million payment deferred for one year, according to the press release. This solidifies their future margins from this very high-potential silver project that should ramp up production significantly from now on. By 2028, this is expected to become the company’s largest source of production from 2027, so it’s likely a good move to do while they can afford it thanks to the high commodity prices. As they said, this would “generate meaningful returns on our investment by lowering La Preciosa's operating cost profile” and allow them to retain as much of the project’s value.

Financially, based on their latest report, the company remains in a very good position, with the cash alone covering the total liabilities, which is an excellent position to be in. This offers a ton of flexibility, and with rate cuts already underway, this brings even more optionality for them to issue lower-cost debt and develop in the future - potentially buying another mine if there’s significant value in that.

They are also advancing their drilling programs, with the objective of testing the downdip extension of the Avino vein system below the current lowest mining level, while a second drill was deployed at the La Preciosa mine to confirm prior results from previous operators. We’ll get an update to their resource base estimation in Q1 2026, but it’s not impossible to see them “strike gold” (or silver in this case) and announce some excellent drill results that boost the market’s confidence, which is something to keep in mind about miners in general. They also said that in Q1 2026:

Avino is planning on releasing its first mineral reserve estimate at the same time as the company has now met the requirements for producing issuer under the National Instrument 43-101 standards of disclosure for mineral projects.

This can help the market gain some more certainty in how it values the company, and should be a good step forward, especially when paired with their goal of becoming Mexico’s next intermediate primary silver producer through their very significant organic growth pipeline.

Macro Changes

The main reason behind the stock’s performance has been silver and gold prices reaching new records (14-year highs for silver, all-time for gold), which, of course, have a very significant impact on ASM’s future results.

Why this is important is because Avino is expecting to become a majority silver producer again from 2026 thanks to their mines ramping up, so the price of silver is going to be crucial for their future. I believe silver is much more tied to reality than gold due to its sources of demand, while gold plays a different role overall, with most (~93%) of its demand mix coming from jewelry, investing, and central banks.

This is why gold’s upside potential is theoretically nearly unlimited, as these sources of demand aren’t the most rational, and we can always see even more hype and fear of missing out boost the price significantly more than the record it just breached. Silver, however, is tied to industrial uses, solar panels, etc., which also depend on the economy, meaning it can continue to benefit from rate cuts, as that unlocks potential for higher demand thanks to a recovery in industrial uses, among others.

As said, the main near-term catalyst here is US rate cuts, which have significant effects on the price of the metals, as we can see since Powell’s Jackson Hole remarks and now with two more cuts expected this year after the one in September. This can allow gold and silver to continue their upcycle, as rate cuts can lead to a weaker dollar, especially if we pair it with the significant political uncertainty around tariffs and fear around a hard landing or even a recession.

And to reiterate my point about calling them “under the radar” despite the significant price increase (as they say, ASM is “the #1 performing silver investment over the past 12 months”), we have to keep in mind that the company doesn’t seem to be that popular when it comes to “big” money. 78% of the shares are still held by retail investors/others, with the company’s management being a top shareholder (~5% in total), on top of various other financial institutions that each hold tiny stakes. This can change a lot if they become a larger (intermediate) miner and get more weight in existing/new funds and ETFs, which can potentially inflate the demand.

Still, risk remains due to their significant exposure to Mexico, which can lead to significant issues for future projects (Mexico has announced that it will halt all new mining concessions in 2025 and also review existing operations - announced in June 23, 2025), higher taxes, environmental issues, etc. If Avino is then forced into paying higher taxes or some environmental damages for some reason, this will obviously affect them significantly, while making new permits tougher to get in the future limits their expansion potential in Mexico. This can also lead to more interest from others in buying Avino, however, as their assets would basically become scarcer, while limiting new permits lowers the supply, which can ultimately benefit the price of silver.

Reiterated Valuation

I’ll keep most of the assumptions unchanged from the previous valuation but adjust for the recent developments, including the number of shares and net cash. Starting with their $17.9 million in free cash flow last year, assuming a 10% higher production but also 45% higher commodity prices (this doesn’t account for the current prices, for a better margin of safety), I’ll start the valuation with $28.55 million in free cash flow, assuming the same level of CAPEX. This FCF then grows at a CAGR that leads to a total increase of 200% by 2029 (meaning an outstanding 31.6%, improved by the recent La Preciosa 100% acquisition - similar to the company’s projections - although not as much if we assume 2024 as the starting year). After 2029, the FCF continues to grow at a 4.5% CAGR until 2034. The terminal growth rate is kept at 2.5%. Using a WACC of 9% to take into account the higher Mexico-related risk, and accounting for $31.83 million net cash, with 154.13 million shares outstanding, this results in an enterprise value of $1.155 billion. After adding net cash, this results in an equity value of about $1.186 billion, or an intrinsic value per share of about $7.69.

Conclusions

Overall, given their excellent financials and organic growth pipeline that’s about to ramp up significantly next year, Avino remains a buy for me. The company remains in an excellent position to take advantage of the current environment, while near- and long-term tailwinds remain very significant, especially if the current commodity prices remain. There is Mexico-related risk that remains unpredictable for now, but the potential reward is also enhanced by rate cuts and tariff uncertainty.

data.title: BTCI: Balancing Yields With NAV Protection Amid Bitcoin Volatility
data.description:
As shown in the orange chart below, it's impressive how quickly the NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BATS:BTCI) has grown, crossing $700 million in AUM (assets under management) since its October 2024 launch. This steady growth in assets contrasts sharply with the highly volatile price action, but it has nonetheless delivered a net upside of around 19% since its inception.

Now, investor sustained interest in the fund is likely due to its unique strategy of generating high monthly income (26% TTM) from Bitcoin's volatility while better protecting against NAV erosion when compared with two peers, as this thesis aims to show. Also, while highlighting the risks, I will make the case for an investment based on the Federal Reserve's monetary policy path and its diversifier potential for crypto exposure in a volatile market.

First, I provide a quick overview of the fund's key details, emphasizing the income factor.

BTCI Generates Income, but its Yield is not the Highest

As an actively managed fund charging 0.98%, it generates income by writing (selling) call options on Bitcoin (BTC-USD) Futures ETFs. In this way, the fund collects premiums, which form the basis for its high monthly distributions. Moreover, as shown below, dividends have been paid regularly, but, unlike some income funds which investors may be familiar with, the amount has varied depending on market conditions. Still, BTCI's core strategy to use Bitcoin's well-known volatility to generate income has worked since its dividend exceeds the median for all ETFs by a whopping 848%.

Looking further, the alternative investment industry has seen the emergence of other such ETFs last year, with one of these being the Roundhill Bitcoin Covered Call Strategy ETF (YBTC), which pays much better yields of 43.8% every week, as shown in the comparison table below. There is also the YieldMax Bitcoin Option Income Strategy ETF (YBIT), which pays an appetizing 53.8% monthly.

Now, each of these income-oriented ETFs has its own strategies to generate income, and their outcomes (yield) also depend on specific volatility conditions to work best, but this comparison shows that for those prioritizing yields, BTCI does not come first. Still, the AUM comparison shows that more money has flowed towards the NEOS fund, at least three and five times YBTC and YBIT, respectively, despite it being incepted much later.

Therefore, the reason for BTCI's appeal cannot be just the yields.

BTCI Provides Better NAV Protection

Diving deeper into the comparison, one notices that the NEOS strategy has fared better in terms of price performance (or NAV performance since we are comparing ETFs), both during the short and long terms, as shown below. Interestingly, these periods comprise both crypto appreciations or depreciations, and show BTCI has performed better during different market conditions.

Without going into the details for the other two funds, and considering its above-25% yield, this means that NEOS's data-driven strategy to maintain exposure to Bitcoin futures, as pictured below, has worked best when it comes to balancing yields with price protection.

Now, some will argue that an eleven-month period is too short to assess the price performance, but as shown in the chart below, market conditions have varied extremely, with Bitcoin making new highs (after gaining more than 50% from April to July) or suffering extreme volatility (dropping by about 30% from January to July).

I further support the fact that BTCI offers the best yield to NAV protection balance using total returns, which is the price appreciation plus the dividends if reinvested. Thus, as shown below, NEOS has demonstrated the best total returns (56.3%), followed by Roundhill (52.8%), with YieldMax coming in a distant third.

Now, to be fair to YieldMax, as its very name suggests, it aims to maximize the yield, but for those who are mindful of NAV erosion, this represents a risk better avoided for an already volatile asset class. Talking risks, the next step is to show that, despite its superior total returns, NEOS is not immune to severe drawdowns.

The Risks Involved with BTCI's Strategy

In this respect, the chart below shows that while BTCI shadows (tracks) BTC, it does not exactly replicate crypto's price movements. Analyzing an upside scenario, NEOS caps the fund's upside potential during sharp Bitcoin price rallies. Hence, when Bitcoin's futures price rises significantly, the fund may not capture all of those gains because of the options strategy.

Looking into the modus operandi, NEOS utilizes a “synthetic covered call strategy” for exposure to the Bitcoin Futures ETF, which also limits its participation in the rise of the crypto beyond a certain point. In other words, if Bitcoin surges sharply, the call options get exercised, denying BTCI the ability to fully participate in its underlying asset's appreciation. This can be frustrating for growth investors wanting maximum gains out of Bitcoin's rallies, as provided by spot ETFs.

Analyzing a downside scenario, the premiums collected by writing call options act like a buffer to partially offset (soften) the downside when Bitcoin drops. However, this only happens during mild volatility, not during sharp price drops. Thus, as shown by the blue chart above closely shadowing the orange one during the January to April period, both BTC and BTCI lost around 40% showing that the option strategy was not that effective in cushioning against volatility when the underlying asset's drawdown is severe.

Taking into consideration the above risks, BTCI is good for high-income seekers who are also prepared to sacrifice part of the upside. Moreover, taking into consideration the fund's vulnerability to severe drawdowns since its price performance is tied to Bitcoin, it is important to assess how the latter is performing.

Bitcoin Volatility May Continue, and BTCI is a Good Diversifier

As seen by the price action during the last five days, when it lost more than 3%, crypto's value can change very quickly and by a large percentage, which makes it risky to invest in, especially for those who cannot stomach risks. Thus, after reaching a high of $123K on August 12, investor appetite for BTC appears to have cooled off despite the Federal Reserve cutting rates by 25 basis points, notably the first dovish action this year.

Now, Bitcoin’s price normally moves inversely to interest rates, meaning when rates go down, Bitcoin tends to rise as this increases liquidity in the monetary system. Thus, in the immediate aftermath of the rate cut on September 17, the price did appreciate to $117K, but the move was temporary, and subsequently, it traded at about $113.5K and at the time of writing, it was at approximately $110K. This drop coincided with the U.S. GDP for the second quarter growing stronger than was expected, meaning the Fed may not need to stimulate economic growth by lowering rates. Hence, in case rates stay at this level for longer, risk assets, including stocks and crypto, may not get sufficient of a boost. Another factor that raised the volatility level was the at least $22 billion in crypto options set to expire by the end of this week.

In these circumstances, BTC price action is likely to fluctuate, especially in the case of inflation becoming sticky, forcing the Fed to remain restrictive. However, at the same time, the current U.S Administration, seen as pro-Bitcoin, aims to make America the "crypto capital of the world". Concrete measures include encouraging digital asset adoption and reducing regulatory uncertainty, hence making it easier to launch new crypto products. At the same time, institutional adoption is on the rise.

Therefore, these factors can provide support to Bitcoin and avoid severe drawdowns, which could be detrimental to BTCI's NAV.

BTCI is also Appropriate as a Bitcoin Diversifier

In these conditions, I have a hold position on BTCI, which is also due to momentum indicators. With BTC's value situated below the 10-day, 50-day, and 100-day moving averages, the downside pressure could continue. However, with the RSI at 43, not oversold (<30) and not overbought (>70), this implies weakness, but not necessarily a sharp selloff. Also, the price could slide further from around $57 to the $47-$48 support level, constituting an opportunity to buy.

So, the technicals could point to a downside drift, not a sharp drawdown, which is actually favorable for BTCI’s option-led income generation. In this scenario, the dividends generated lead to better total returns, thereby lessening the gap with BTC. For investors, the chart below shows how the gap between BTCI and BTC has narrowed to around 9% compared to 45% when only price returns are considered (above chart).

In conclusion, this thesis has shown that for those wanting income while being mindful of NAV erosion, BTCI's strategy provides the best balance between yields and price performance, or NAV depreciation in this particular case. Moreover, given the volatility risks associated with this asset class, it may be better for those who already hold Bitcoin through an ETF and are contemplating putting more money into cryptos to diversify their exposure using the NEOS option strategy.

To this end, compared to pure growth available through spot ETFs, BTCI presents a strong case involving generating income from volatility, making it suitable as a complementary holding in a crypto portfolio. In this connection, I calculated that BTCI's asset flow has accelerated by 600% (from around $100 million to $700 million in the introductory chart), just after Bitcoin’s dip. By comparison, the cumulative inflows into Bitcoin Spot ETFs, while still positive, have experienced greater daily variability during this time period. Also, periods of outflows have started to emerge, like in August, coinciding with spot Ethereum (ETH-USD) ETFs seeing heightened investor interest.

Well, this could be transitory and but income is a key factor that supports BTCI. It's above 25% source of yield, which already dwarfs those of classical equity and bond funds, will become even more attractive as interest rates on savings and cash equivalents decline in case the Fed carries out two further cuts by the end of this year, as expected.

data.title: Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Core Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Broad U.S. equity markets experienced significant volatility in the second quarter, largely driven by the burgeoning trade war. Markets dropped over 10% in early April following the U.S. “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, then surged to new highs by late June as tariff concerns eased among investors. A momentum-driven headwind prevailed, driving lower-quality, higher-volatility stocks to generally outperform higher-quality, lower-volatility stocks—penalizing the kinds of stocks KAR focuses on, which offer the potential for growth with a smoother ride over the long term.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Core Fund returned 3.36% (MUTF:VKSIX) (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell 2500 TM Index, which returned 8.59%. Stock selection in the industrials and healthcare sectors detracted from performance, while underweights in the real estate and energy sectors contributed positively to performance.

Bentley Systems (BSY) and Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Bentley Systems is a software development company specializing in infrastructure engineering software. The company reported consistent profitable growth, particularly in software subscriptions, as the business benefits from a strengthening infrastructure spending environment.
  • Interactive Brokers, a global online brokerage firm, reported continued strong growth in client assets and number of client accounts, with continued strength in Asia and Europe. This strength in client growth drove growth in the company’s revenues and profits.

Charles River Laboratories (CRL) and Saia (SAIA) were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Charles River, a research and drug development company, was impacted by a recent Food and Drug Administration (FDA) announcement about a planned shift from animal testing in medical research. We believe this creates a structural change in the company’s business model, and we sold our position.
  • Saia is a transportation company specializing in less-than-truckload (LTL) freight shipping. The company’s share price declined after it reported a decline in profits. Near-term profitability is being pressured by the added costs of new terminals opened last year and a still weak shipping environment, which may worsen over the coming months due to tariff uncertainty.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, we purchased Hamilton Lane (HLNE) for the portfolio. We sold Charles River Laboratories (discussed above), Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG), and Teradyne (TER).

  • Hamilton Lane is a holding company that engages in the provision of private markets investment solutions. The firm works with clients to conceive, structure, build out, manage, and monitor portfolios of private markets funds and direct investments. It also offers customized separate accounts, specialized funds, advisory services, distribution management, and reporting, monitoring, data, and analytics.
  • We sold Charles River in response to structural changes we believe impact its business. On April 10, the FDA announced plans to phase out animal testing for monoclonal antibodies and other drugs. Although the shift to non-animal testing is expected to be gradual and Charles River could benefit from artificial intelligence (AI)-based testing models it is developing, that time frame may be several years out.
  • We sold our shares in Scotts Miracle-Gro due to the lawn and garden care company’s lack of consistent capital allocation discipline. We became particularly concerned when Scotts announced the unexpected departure of its CFO. In view of the company’s large family ownership/board representation, we believe Scotts needs a strong independent voice that the current chair/CEO (son of the company’s founder) does not provide. When it became clear that the chief accounting officer serving as interim CFO was to be named to the permanent CFO role, that ruined our hopes for a strong replacement.
  • We sold our underweight position in Teradyne, a manufacturer of automated test equipment. We grew increasingly disappointed with the company’s capital allocation decisions particularly as it relates to its continuous investment in the inherently inferior, highly competitive industrial automation (robotics) business.
